title: Quiz: SPHINCS+ Stateless Signatures
description: Test your understanding of SPHINCS+ architecture and stateless signature principles

questions.question: What is the main advantage of SPHINCS+ over XMSS/LMS?
questions.explanation: SPHINCS+ is stateless - it doesn't need to track which keys have been used. This eliminates risks from state loss, backup/restore issues, and multi-device signing. The tradeoff is larger signatures.

questions.question: What is a hypertree in SPHINCS+?

options.text: A very tall single Merkle tree
Multiple layers of Merkle trees stacked together
A compressed representation of all public keys

questions.explanation: A hypertree consists of d layers of Merkle trees. Each tree at layer i authenticates the roots of trees at layer i-1. This allows a virtual tree of height h without storing 2^h keys.

questions.question: What is the purpose of FORS in SPHINCS+?

options.text: To sign the message digest efficiently

questions.explanation: FORS (Forest of Random Subsets) is a few-time signature scheme used to sign the message digest. It's more efficient than WOTS+ for this purpose, using k trees where each signature reveals one leaf per tree.

questions.question: How does SPHINCS+ achieve statelessness with a virtual tree of 2^64 leaves?

options.text: It pre-computes all leaves during key generation
It uses the message hash to pseudo-randomly select a leaf
It stores only a compressed tree

questions.explanation: SPHINCS+ derives the leaf index pseudo-randomly from the message hash. This means the same message always uses the same leaf, but different messages use different (random-looking) leaves. No tracking needed.

questions.question: What does the 's' vs 'f' suffix mean in SPHINCS+ parameter sets (e.g., 128s vs 128f)?
questions.explanation: 's' variants optimize for smaller signatures (more hypertree layers, more computation). 'f' variants optimize for faster signing (fewer layers, larger signatures). Example: SLH-DSA-128s ~8KB, SLH-DSA-128f ~17KB.

questions.question: In FORS with parameters k=14 and a=12, how many leaves are selected to sign a message?
questions.explanation: FORS has k=14 trees, each with 2^a = 2^12 leaves. The message hash is split into k chunks of a bits each, and each chunk selects one leaf from its corresponding tree. So 14 leaves total.

questions.question: What is the security foundation of SPHINCS+?

options.text: Hash function properties only (PRF, preimage resistance)

questions.explanation: SPHINCS+ security relies entirely on standard hash function properties: PRF security, second-preimage resistance, and target collision resistance. No structured mathematical problems are assumed, making it the most conservative choice.

questions.question: Why is FORS called a 'few-time' rather than 'one-time' signature?

options.text: It can only sign a few messages total
It can safely sign the same message multiple times
Each tree can be used a few times

questions.explanation: Unlike WOTS+ where any reuse is catastrophic, FORS can safely sign the same message multiple times (producing the same signature). Different messages are safe until birthday collision (~2^(ka/2) signatures).

questions.question: SPHINCS+ signatures are typically smaller than Dilithium signatures.
questions.explanation: False! SPHINCS+ signatures are much larger. Dilithium (ML-DSA-65) signatures are ~3.3 KB, while SPHINCS+ ranges from ~8 KB (128s) to ~50 KB (256f). This is the tradeoff for statelessness and minimal assumptions.
